key_insight: Raising argon flow from 1600 to 1800 L/h during air plasma spraying produces denser NiCrFeBSiC coatings with greater thickness, lower porosity, and better tribological performance.
engineering_relevance: Optimizing plasma gas flow is a simple process lever to improve coating quality, wear resistance, and adhesion for self-fluxing metal-ceramic protective layers.
method: Air plasma sprayed NiCrFeBSiC coatings at two argon flow rates: 1600 and 1800 L/h
Characterized structure, microhardness, elastic modulus, friction, wear, and adhesion
Compared ball-on-disk tribology and scratch-test failure behavior
evidence_signal: At 1800 L/h, coating thickness roughly doubled, porosity dropped by about half, and the friction coefficient decreased to about 0.107 while scratch adhesion remained strong.
watch_item: The abstract compares only two argon flow settings, so broader process windows and long-term service durability are not established.
paper_title: EFFECT OF ARGON FLOW RATE DURING AIR PLASMA SPRAYING ON THE STRUCTURE AND PROPERTIES OF NICRFEBSIC COATINGS
